one_line_takeaway: By aggregating fine-tuned models over relative (rather than raw) probability distributions and smoothing all but the top-m most-informative tokens toward a shared base model, SCP-Δr turns NAF-based defenses from theoretically loose and empirically leaky (CP-Δ, NAS 0.47-0.76) into a provably and empirically strong training-data-extraction defense (SCP-Δr-b, NAS 0.11) with no measurable utility cost.
key_finding: SCP-Δr-b achieves the lowest overall Normalized Attack Score (NAS) of 0.11, versus 0.99 for No CP, 0.47 for CP-Δ, 0.76 for CP-ΔKL, 0.50 for CP-Fuse, 0.22 for CP-Δr (its own un-smoothed predecessor), and 0.17 for the SCP-Δr-c variant, while a held-out reference lower bound (No CP-val) sits at NAS=0.002. On canary exposure (mean, All datasets), SCP-Δr-b scores 1.4 vs 8.8 (CP-Δ), 23 (CP-ΔKL), 3.8 (CP-Fuse), and 3.6 (CP-Δr). On PII extraction (Names2IDs), SCP-Δr-b achieves Full Extraction Rate (FER) <0.001 versus 0.15 (CP-Δ) and 0.06 (CP-Δr). Theoretically, at the 99th percentile the NAF bound kx is 0.004 for SCP-Δr versus 0.98 for CP-Δr and 11.6 for CP-Δ (orders-of-magnitude improvement). Utility (next-token validation accuracy, mean across datasets) is essentially flat across methods (0.37-0.42), with SCP-Δr-b actually highest at 0.72 on the reported 'Utility' column, and validation accuracy stable at 0.495±0.005 for smoothing parameter m in {10,100,1000}, degrading only at m=1 (0.39). An ablation applying CP-Δ (raw probabilities) to the smoothed distributions instead of CP-Δr's relative-probability aggregation yields canary scores (mean 8.1, 95th pct 18.3) comparable to unsmoothed CP-Δ, showing that RPD-based aggregation, not smoothing alone, is necessary for the protection gain.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SCP-Δr, a Near-Access-Freeness (NAF)-based defense that protects fine-tuned LLMs from training-data-extraction (TDE) attacks with provable bounds and negligible utility loss. It (1) defines the SpaRPS (Sparse Relative Probability Shift) property, an idealized structural condition capturing when fine-tuning-induced probability shifts are concentrated on a small token subset, and shows empirically (via a new 'Expected SpaRPS' relaxation measured over 16 independently fine-tuned LLaMA2-7B seeds on MathAbstracts) that real fine-tuned models do NOT satisfy it naturally; (2) proposes SCP-Δr, which combines relative probability distributions (RPDs, following prior work by the same first author, Segal et al. 2025) with a novel base-model smoothing step that explicitly enforces SpaRPS by replacing all but the top-m highest-scoring tokens' relative probabilities with those of a shared base model before CP-style aggregation; (3) proves new theorems (4.4, 4.5, Proposition 4.8) connecting NAF/CP-style bounds to a formal black-box TDE game and a likelihood-ratio-attack (LiRA) framework, showing CP-Δ (the existing raw-probability NAF baseline) yields bounds too loose to meaningfully constrain extraction, whereas SCP-Δr yields provably small bounds under stated assumptions; and (4) empirically evaluates SCP-Δr against existing NAF-based methods (CP-Δ, CP-ΔKL, CP-Fuse, and CP-Δr) using canary exposure, PII extraction, and a newly proposed adaptive token-by-token-extraction (TTE) attack, plus a novel practical extraction attack against NAF-protected models.
threat_model: Black-box TDE adversary (Definition 4.2): given a set of prefixes of targeted training examples and black-box query access to the target model's per-token output probabilities, plus white-box access to a reference model trained similarly on similar (but non-target) data, the adversary predicts the next token for each prefix (with abstention allowed) to reconstruct/verify training examples. The optimal attack strategy is characterized as a likelihood-ratio attack (LiRA), thresholding r1(y|x)/r0(y|x). The defense assumes a standard NAF partition structure: for any sensitive example, at least one of two constituent models trained on disjoint dataset partitions was not trained on that example (or its semantic equivalent); the paper explicitly flags near-duplicate/overlapping-partition training data as an open limitation.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) guarantees rely on the standard NAF partition assumption that for any sensitive example at least one constituent model was not trained on it or a semantic equivalent; extending to near-duplicate/overlapping training data is left as future work, though the paper notes prior work (Abad et al. 2025) found CP-style methods retain protection with up to 33% partition overlap. (2) The extraction-bound-to-TDE-advantage interpretation depends on LiRA being (near-)optimal for the adversary; the authors state it is an open question in which regimes this assumption holds and whether stronger attacks exist when it does not. (3) SpaRPS/Expected SpaRPS do not hold naturally for fine-tuned LLMs (empirically demonstrated in Section 3.2 with a Figure 1a analysis: even allowing 1024 unconstrained token shifts, 10% of prompts still show large deviation, B≈2.1) -- SCP-Δr's smoothing enforces the property rather than relying on it holding intrinsically, and the paper frames its no-utility-loss claim from smoothing as a conjecture validated empirically rather than proven. (4) The paper treats extension of SCP-Δr principles to pre-training as future work, noting pre-training differs in scale, optimization dynamics, and data heterogeneity. (5) Empirical attacks used in Section 5.3 do not explicitly implement LiRA (they are argued to approximate it with a near-ideal reference model, discussed in an appendix) rather than being a literal LiRA instantiation.

datasets_used: MathAbstracts (Zhang et al. 2025b) - paper titles and abstracts, fine-tuned on LLaMA2-7B
StoryTelling (Fan et al. 2018) - story topics and stories, fine-tuned on LLaMA2-7B
CodeInstructions (Morales 2023) - instructions and Python solutions, fine-tuned on StarCoder-7B
Names2IDs - synthetic dataset of names paired with 10-digit IDs, created by the authors for PII-extraction evaluation, fine-tuned on LLaMA2-7B
quantitative_results: Overall NAS (Normalized Attack Score, lower=better): No CP 0.99, CP-Δ 0.47, CP-ΔKL 0.76, CP-Fuse 0.50, CP-Δr 0.22, SCP-Δr-c 0.17, SCP-Δr-b 0.11, No CP-val (reference lower bound) 0.002
Canary exposure mean (All datasets, lower=better): No CP 28, CP-Δ 8.8, CP-ΔKL 23, CP-Fuse 3.8, CP-Δr 3.6, SCP-Δr-c 1.6, SCP-Δr-b 1.4
PII Full Extraction Rate (FER, Names2IDs): No CP 0.94, CP-Δ 0.15, CP-ΔKL 0.34, CP-Fuse 0.34, CP-Δr 0.06, SCP-Δr-c 0.05, SCP-Δr-b <0.001
PII Average Extracted Length (AEL, Names2IDs): No CP 7.4, CP-Δ 2.4, CP-ΔKL 4.1, CP-Fuse 4.1, CP-Δr 1.9, SCP-Δr-c 1.8, SCP-Δr-b 0.43
Utility (mean accuracy across datasets, higher=better): No CP 0.67, CP-Δ 0.70, CP-ΔKL 0.70, CP-Fuse 0.68, CP-Δr 0.69, SCP-Δr-c 0.69, SCP-Δr-b 0.72
99th-percentile NAF bound kx: CP-Δ 11.6, CP-Δr 0.98, SCP-Δr 0.004 (orders-of-magnitude improvement per step)
Validation accuracy vs smoothing parameter m: m=1 degrades to 0.39; m in {10,100,1000} stable at 0.495±0.005
baselines_compared: No CP (single fine-tuned model, no NAF aggregation)
CP-Δ (Vyas et al. 2023) - standard NAF pointwise-minimum aggregation on raw probabilities
CP-ΔKL (Vyas et al. 2023) - NAF variant under KL divergence
CP-Fuse (Abad et al. 2025) - fusing strategy proposed to mitigate CP-ΔKL generation drift
CP-Δr (Segal et al. 2025) - the authors' own prior relative-probability aggregation without smoothing
No CP-val - held-out validation-set reference used as an approximate lower bound on achievable attack scores
